input.content:
## So the meter isn't perfect. What's it missing?

Rebuild it in your head for a second. Two arrows go in, one number comes out. Multiply the matching pieces, then add those products up. That's the whole meter. Simple enough to rebuild from memory, which is usually a good sign.

Except simple isn't the same as complete. Take a candidate that already points the exact same direction as our target, and just stretch it, make every piece bigger without changing where it points. The score climbs. Nothing about the direction changed, but the meter reads higher anyway.

That's a real problem, not a technicality. A long, sprawling answer can out-score a short, precise one for no reason other than its size. A long song would beat a short one on sheer loudness, even if the short one matches the target's shape better. The meter is currently measuring "how much" tangled up with "which way," and it can't tell you which part of the reading is doing the work.

> What's the cheapest fix that keeps the direction the score is measuring but forgets how big the arrows were to begin with?

Sit with that one. We're not answering it here: it's the question that opens the next session. For now, just notice the gap. The meter we've got tells you direction and size at once, and that mixture is exactly what we'll learn to pull apart.

input.content:
## So what does the meter actually compute?

Let's rebuild it from memory, since you've now watched it work on songs and on a job posting. Two arrows come in. You match up their entries, multiply each pair, then add all those products into one number. That's it. That's the whole meter: multiply, then add.

Notice what that meter never asked. It never asked what the numbers meant. Song taste, resume keywords, anything with two lists of numbers the same length: the multiply-and-add doesn't care about the subject, only the shape. That's exactly why it travels so well from aisle to aisle.

But traveling well isn't the same as getting things right, and here's the crack worth staring at. Take a candidate's resume and stretch it, repeat the same skills, pad the list, without changing what they're actually good at. Their score climbs anyway. Nothing about their direction moved. Only the length did.

Same problem with songs: a track that's simply louder in every frequency racks up a bigger score than a quieter track with the exact same taste profile. The raw multiply-and-add rewards size right alongside direction, and it can't tell you which one did the work.

> What's the cheapest fix that keeps the direction the meter is sensing but forgets about the size doing the shouting?

Sit with that on the way out. You don't need the answer yet. You need the shape of the question, because that gap between "direction" and "size" is exactly where the next session picks up.

result: DONE So what does the meter actually compute?

input.content:
## So does bigger just mean better?

Let's stress-test the meter we just rebuilt. Same recipe every time: multiply matching entries, add up the products, get one number. That's why it travels so well between wildly different aisles. Swap the songs for a job posting, score a candidate against what the role wants, and you're doing the identical operation: multiply the matched entries, add them up. The math never asked what the numbers meant, which is exactly why it doesn't care whether they're loudness levels or years of experience.

That portability is the whole appeal. It's also where the trouble starts.

> If a candidate's numbers just get bigger, same direction, longer arrow, does its score change?

It climbs. Every single time. Stretch a candidate's arrow without changing its direction at all, an exaggerated "5 years" where the truth was "3," an inflated confidence score, and the meter goes up anyway. The direction, the thing that was supposed to represent fit, never moved. The score climbed on size alone.

Same story back in the song aisle: a longer track, more notes, more everything, racks up a bigger score even if its taste in genres is identical to a shorter one. Long wins on sheer loudness. Padded resumes win on sheer length. The meter we built to measure alignment is quietly rewarding volume instead, and it never tells you which one did the work.

That's not a bug we can shrug off, and it's not a reason to distrust the whole idea either. It's a gap with a shape: the raw score bundles direction together with size, and we need a way to keep the direction while forgetting the size. That question, the cheapest fix that does exactly that, is where we pick back up next.

result: DONE So does bigger just mean better?

input.content:
## What happens if you just make one arrow bigger?

We've been trusting this meter to size up similarity, so let's actually test it. Take c1, keep its direction exactly the same, and just stretch it: twice as long, pointing the same way it always did.

> If you double c1's length exactly, same direction, just twice as long, does the number double too?

It does. Exactly. Not roughly, not "trending that way": the score doubles right along with the length. That's because the meter is linear in each arrow you feed it, multiply and add, nothing fancier hiding underneath. Feed it twice the input, get twice the output, every time.

This shows up outside of songs, too. Picture scoring a resume against a job posting: multiply each matched skill by how much the role wants it, add those up, and you get one fit number. Same meter, different aisle. It doesn't know or care what the numbers represent, direction, volume, years of experience. That indifference is exactly why it travels so well from one problem to the next.

But indifference cuts both ways. A long, padded resume can out-score a lean, well-matched one on sheer length alone. A loud song can out-score a quieter, better-matched one on sheer volume. The meter isn't confused: it's doing exactly what multiply-and-add does. Size passes straight through, and right now nothing stops it.

So here's the question worth sitting with on the walk home: if size scales the score in lockstep like this, what's the cheapest way to keep the direction the meter is reading while forgetting the size that's currently along for the ride?

input.content:
## What happens if you just make one arrow bigger?

We've been trusting this meter to size up similarity, so let's actually test it. Take c1, keep its direction exactly the same, and just stretch it: twice as long, pointing the same way it always did.

> If you double c1's length exactly, same direction, just twice as long, does the number double too?

It does. Exactly. Not roughly, not "trending that way": the score doubles right along with the length. That's because the meter is linear in each arrow you feed it, multiply and add, nothing fancier hiding underneath. Feed it twice the input, get twice the output, every time.

This shows up outside of songs, too. Picture scoring a resume against a job posting: multiply each matched skill by how much the role wants it, add those up, and you get one fit number. Same meter, different aisle. It doesn't know or care what the numbers represent, direction, volume, years of experience. That indifference is exactly why it travels so well from one problem to the next.

But indifference cuts both ways. A long, padded resume can out-score a lean, well-matched one on sheer length alone. A loud song can out-score a quieter, better-matched one on sheer volume. The meter isn't confused: it's doing exactly what multiply-and-add does. Size passes straight through, and right now nothing stops it.

So here's the question worth sitting with on the walk home: if size scales the score in lockstep like this, what's the cheapest way to keep the direction the meter is reading while forgetting the size that's currently along for the ride?

> If you double c1's length exactly, same direction, just twice as long, does the number double too?

This shows up outside of songs, too. Picture scoring a resume against a job posting: multiply each matched skill by how much the role wants it, add those up, and you get one fit number. Same meter, different aisle. It doesn't know or care what the numbers represent, direction, volume, years of experience. lines: -## So does bigger just mean better?
+## What happens if you just make one arrow bigger?

-Let's stress-test the meter we just rebuilt. Same recipe every time: multiply matching entries, add up the products, get one number. That's why it travels so well between wildly different aisles. So let's keep the same meter running and walk it one aisle over. We don't rebuild anything, we just point the needle at a job posting instead of a song. Score a candidate against what the role wants and you're doing the identical operation: multiply the matched entries, add them up. The math never asked what the numbers meant, which is exactly why it doesn't care whether they're loudness levels or years of experience.

+We've been trusting this meter to size up similarity, so let's actually test it. Take c1, keep its direction exactly the same, and just stretch it: twice as long, pointing the same way it always did.
-That portability is the whole appeal. It's also where the trouble starts.
+> If you double c1's length exactly, same direction, just twice as long, does the number double too?
-> If you double a candidate's arrow exactly, same direction, just twice as long, does the number double too?

+It does. Exactly. Not roughly, not "trending that way": the score doubles right along with the length. That's because the meter is linear in each arrow you feed it, multiply and add, nothing fancier hiding underneath. Feed it twice the input, get twice the output, every time.

-It does, exactly. Double the arrow, double the score, every single time. The meter is linear in each input: scale one arrow by some amount and the number scales by that same amount. An exaggerated "5 years" where the truth was "3," an inflated confidence score, and the meter climbs in lockstep. The direction, the thing that was supposed to represent fit, never moved. The score climbed on size alone.

+This shows up outside of songs, too. Picture scoring a resume against a job posting: multiply each matched skill by how much the role wants it, add those up, and you get one fit number. Same meter, different aisle. It doesn't know or care what the numbers represent, direction, volume, years of experience. That indifference is exactly why it travels so well from one problem to the next.

-Same story back in the song aisle: a longer track, more notes, more everything, racks up a bigger score even if its taste in genres is identical to a shorter one. Long wins on sheer loudness. Padded resumes win on sheer length. The meter we built to measure alignment is quietly rewarding volume instead, and it never tells you which one did the work.

+But indifference cuts both ways. A long, padded resume can out-score a lean, well-matched one on sheer length alone. A loud song can out-score a quieter, better-matched one on sheer volume. The meter isn't confused: it's doing exactly what multiply-and-add does. Size passes straight through, and right now nothing stops it.

-That's not a bug we can shrug off, and it's not a reason to distrust the whole idea either. It's a gap with a shape: the raw score bundles direction together with size, and we need a way to keep the direction while forgetting the size. That question, the cheapest fix that does exactly that, is where we pick back up next.

+So here's the question worth sitting with on the walk home: if size scales the score in lockstep like this, what's the cheapest way to keep the direction the meter is reading while forgetting the size that's currently along for the ride?

tool_use_result.originalFile:
## So does bigger just mean better?

Let's stress-test the meter we just rebuilt. Same recipe every time: multiply matching entries, add up the products, get one number. That's why it travels so well between wildly different aisles. So let's keep the same meter running and walk it one aisle over. We don't rebuild anything, we just point the needle at a job posting instead of a song. Score a candidate against what the role wants and you're doing the identical operation: multiply the matched entries, add them up. The math never asked what the numbers meant, which is exactly why it doesn't care whether they're loudness levels or years of experience.

That portability is the whole appeal. It's also where the trouble starts.

> If you double a candidate's arrow exactly, same direction, just twice as long, does the number double too?

It does, exactly. Double the arrow, double the score, every single time. The meter is linear in each input: scale one arrow by some amount and the number scales by that same amount. An exaggerated "5 years" where the truth was "3," an inflated confidence score, and the meter climbs in lockstep. The direction, the thing that was supposed to represent fit, never moved. The score climbed on size alone.

Same story back in the song aisle: a longer track, more notes, more everything, racks up a bigger score even if its taste in genres is identical to a shorter one. Long wins on sheer loudness. Padded resumes win on sheer length. The meter we built to measure alignment is quietly rewarding volume instead, and it never tells you which one did the work.

That's not a bug we can shrug off, and it's not a reason to distrust the whole idea either. It's a gap with a shape: the raw score bundles direction together with size, and we need a way to keep the direction while forgetting the size. That question, the cheapest fix that does exactly that, is where we pick back up next.

content.text: DONE What happens if you just make one arrow bigger?
